ProPublica Illinois是一个调查滥用权力的非营利性新闻编辑室。注册以获取每周有关我们工作的最新信息。
伊利诺伊州的学校不需要教授该州历史上的一门课。也许结果是，当你得知伊利诺伊州曾经允许奴隶制时，你可能会感到惊讶。但达雷尔·德克斯特(Darrel Dexter)是塔姆斯的一名历史学家和高中社会研究教师，多年来一直在研究、撰写和教授伊利诺伊州的奴隶史。他关于这个主题的书“埃及的奴役，南伊利诺伊州的奴隶制”(Condage in Egypia，Slavery in Southern Illinois)于2011年由东南密苏里州立大学出版，一位学术评论家称这本书是“迄今为止对伊利诺伊州种族奴隶制最全面的描述”。我第一次见到德克斯特是在伊利诺伊州安娜的公共图书馆，当时我正在报道该市作为日落小镇的历史。从那时起，他帮助我更好地了解了我们州的历史中经常在课堂课程中遗漏的部分，因此，也帮助了公众对伊利诺伊州历史的理解。
最近，就在纪念奴隶制结束的6月19日假期之前，我与德克斯特进行了交谈。以下是我们谈话的编辑版本。
在了解南北战争的过程中，我们通常了解到有“奴隶州”和“自由州”。伊利诺伊州在哪些方面使这些看似明确的定义变得复杂？
伊利诺伊州是我所说的准奴隶州。令人惊讶的是，它是在法律范围内存在的。它始于来自法国的殖民奴隶法(因为伊利诺伊州是法国领土)。因此，奴隶制，至少是对非洲人的奴役，后来是对非洲裔美国人的奴役，至少早在1720年，甚至可能在此之前，就开始在法国的定居点开始。然后，“西北条例”(该法案为大湖区的大部分地区提供了加入联邦的途径)规定，除作为对犯罪的惩罚外，不应存在奴隶制、契约奴役或非自愿奴役。所以人们似乎在想，“好吧，奴隶制就到此为止了。”但人们仍然把奴隶带进来，这是违法的。
note_en:
ProPublica Illinois is a nonprofit newsroom that investigates abuses of power. Sign up to get  weekly updates about our work.
  Illinois schools are not required to teach a class in the state’s history. Perhaps as a result, you may be surprised to learn that Illinois at one time allowed slavery. But Darrel Dexter, a historian and high school social studies teacher in Tamms, has been researching, writing about and teaching the history of slavery in Illinois for years. His book about the topic, “Bondage in Egypt, Slavery in Southern Illinois,” was published by Southeast Missouri State University in 2011, and  described by one academic reviewer as “the most comprehensive account to date of racial slavery in Illinois.” I first met Dexter at the public library in Anna, Illinois, while reporting on its history as a sundown town. Since then, he’s helped me better understand the parts of our state’s history often left out of classroom curricula and, as a result, the public’s understanding of Illinois history.
  I recently spoke with Dexter just ahead of the Juneteenth holiday, which commemorates the ending of slavery. Here is an edited version of our conversation.
  In learning about the Civil War, we typically learn there were “slave states” and “free states.” In what ways does Illinois complicate those seemingly clear-cut definitions?
  Illinois is what I would call a quasi-slave state. And what’s surprising to people is that it existed here within the law. It begins with the colonial slave laws that came from France (because Illinois was a French territory). And so slavery, at least the enslavement of Africans and then later African American people, started in the French settlements, at least as early as 1720, maybe even before that. And then the Northwest Ordinance (an act that provided a path for much of the Great Lakes region to be admitted to the Union) stated that there shall be neither slavery nor indentured servitude, nor involuntary servitude, except as the punishment of crime. And so people seem to think, “Well, that does it for slavery.” But people still brought slaves in, in violation of the law.
title: 伊利诺伊州存在奴隶制，但学校并不总是教授那段历史
title_en: Slavery existed in Illinois, but schools don’t always teach that history

note:
这是对低端武器化业余爱好无人机构成的威胁以及它们将变得多么普遍的又一次警醒。
据报道，土耳其军方计划在短期内购买500多架四直升机型卡尔古系列游荡弹药，即自杀式无人机。目前，“卡尔古斯”号可以在半自动或人工控制模式下作战，但目前正在努力让其中多达20个“卡古斯”号拥有作为群体发动大规模攻击的能力，这可能会给土耳其军队带来潜在的改变游戏规则的新能力。
土耳其国营的阿纳多卢通讯社最先报道，国防技术工程和贸易公司，也就是土耳其语的首字母缩写STM，预计将于2020年6月15日向土耳其武装部队交付数百架无人机。目前尚不清楚这份500多架无人机的总订单是否包括或不包括土耳其政府在1月份宣布的356架Kargus的采购协议。
STM在2017年推出了第一代卡尔古，土耳其军方去年首次开始接收少量改进的卡尔古-2变种。据报道，土耳其军队去年在该国与叙利亚边境的行动中至少部署了无人机，如果没有部署的话。
制造商表示，Kargu是专门为反恐和非对称战争场景设计的。Kargu-2重15磅，飞行速度高达每小时90英里，在空中最长可停留30分钟。Kargu-2在土耳其语中的字面意思是鹰，也被用来指小的山上瞭望台。该制造商表示，Kargu-2是专门为反恐和不对称战争场景而设计的，在空中最多可以保持30分钟的飞行速度，在土耳其语中的字面意思是鹰。它有一个视线控制链路，射程约为6英里。
与早期型号相比，卡尔古-2还可以飞得更高，航程更远，并能够在指定区域停留更长时间。它还更新了瞄准能力和改进，以减少其听觉特征，后者有助于降低对手在无人机为时已晚之前发现它的机会。
地面操作员可以手动控制任何一架卡尔古系列无人机，并使用它们的机载传感器，包括光电和红外摄像机以及激光成像系统(LIDAR)，以进行一般监视，识别和跟踪目标。然后，他们可以指挥四架直升机攻击指定的威胁，即使它在移动。如果没有发现目标，游荡的弹药也可以安全地返回操作员重新使用。
这种无人机可以携带三种不同类型的弹头中的一种，包括一种高爆炸破片弹头，一种用于在露天与人员和其他非装甲目标交战的弹头，一种适合建筑物或洞穴等受限空间目标的温压式弹头，以及一种用于攻击轻型装甲威胁的聚能装药。每只重约3磅。无人机的弹头也可以设置为在目标上方撞击或空爆时发挥作用，后者是一个特别适用于破碎型和热压型的功能。
操作者还可以使用卡古斯作为传统的导弹来攻击固定目标。在这种攻击方法中，无人机将使用其GPS导航系统来打击所需的位置。
卡尔古系列无人机还可以在半自动模式下操作，在这种模式下，操作员指挥四翼直升机飞行到某个区域，然后自行探测和交战目标。只要视线控制链路保持未断开，操作员就可以在整个过程中保持在循环中，并可以在必要时改变无人机的方向或中止其攻击。以色列长期以来一直是这类人在回路控制系统的先驱，这种系统现在是大多数自杀式无人机的默认系统，在其他类型的弹药上也变得越来越受欢迎。
然而，最重要的是，去年，STM宣布正在努力赋予卡尔古无人机家族更多的自治权和大规模合作的能力。作为土耳其政府一个名为Kerkes的更大项目的一部分，蜂群技术正在开发中，该项目还在寻求开发系统，以提高无人机在GPS被拒绝的环境中操作的能力，这一点越来越成为一个非常现实的威胁。
目前还不清楚该公司希望在多大程度上实现武装四翼直升机的网络化，但即使能够一次发射多达20架这种直升机的更基本的大规模攻击，也将显著提升其能力。
测试表明，一枚装有空气爆破型高爆炸破片弹头的卡尔古弹头可以有效地与直径约20英尺的圆圈内的人员群交战。不止一个人一起工作，可以在相对较大的区域内均匀地应对威胁。除了对人员集中造成毁灭性的破坏外，这还可能使对其他软目标的快速大规模攻击成为可能，包括轻型车辆车队、停放的飞机、雷达碟形天线和传感器系统、弹药和燃料场等等。有了卡尔古目前可用的不同弹头选项的混合，一组无人机也可能实施更复杂的攻击。
如果成群的卡古斯有能力在预先设定的参数范围内以完全自主的模式工作，它们可能会变得更有能力。从本质上讲，蜂群可以迷惑和压倒对手的防御，即使是那些属于主要军队的防御，即使其中相当多的人在到达目标之前被击落，也会造成严重破坏。这是一个非常现实的威胁，战区过去曾在多个场合进行过详细的探索。
根据它的一般尺寸和配置，Kargus似乎是提供这种能力的一个相对低成本的选择，这也突显了这种蜂群技术的进入门槛正在变得多么低。除了在蜂拥而至的军事发展方面更加强劲之外，以大型协调编队飞行的小型四翼直升机类型的无人机多年来也一直在商业部门使用。
STM正在开发的集群技术也可能适用于它正在开发的其他无人机和游荡弹药。在它的其他产品中，该公司还提供一种固定翼管发射的游荡弹药，名为Alpagu，在形式和功能上与AeroVironment的SwitchBlade非常相似，现在也在土耳其服役。
似乎很有可能的是，除了向土耳其武装部队提供这些改进的卡尔古斯之外，STM还可以寻求出口它们，从而在世界各地进一步扩散这种能力。STM已经表示，它收到了至少三个未透露姓名的潜在外国客户对Kargu系列的认真询问。土耳其作为一个整体，已经成为无人机开发和生产的强国，就在今年，土耳其在叙利亚和利比亚使用了更大的型号，取得了很大的效果。
这正是我们多年来一直警告的武器类型。它已经在这里并有可能出口的事实应该是对低端无人机对美国和盟军以及国内基础设施和贵宾构成的威胁水平的又一次警醒。
上周，美国中央司令部司令、美国海军陆战队上将肯尼思·麦肯齐(Kenneth McKenzie)在中东研究所主办的一次活动上表示：我一直与空军的朋友们争辩说，飞行的未来是垂直的，是无人驾驶的，这是美国海军陆战队上将肯尼斯·麦肯齐(Kenneth McKenzie)上周在中东研究所主办的一次活动上说的。我说的不是大型无人驾驶平台，这是我们可以看到和处理的传统战斗机大小的平台，就像我们可以看到和处理的任何其他平台一样。&#34；我不是在谈论大型无人驾驶平台，它们是我们可以看到和处理的传统战斗机大小的平台。
我说的是现在美国好市多花一千美元就能买到的那种，四轮驱动式的，旋翼机之类的，可以下水和飞行的，他继续说，“我说的是现在美国好市多花一千美元就能买到的那种，四个四轮驱动的，旋翼机之类的，可以下水和飞行的。”通过非常简单的修改，它可以制成可以投掷武器的东西，比如手榴弹或其他东西。
卡古斯及其未来的蜂群能力很可能成为下一个主要的土耳其无人机成功故事，无论是好是坏。
note_en:
This is yet another wake-up call to the threat that low-end weaponized hobby-like drones pose and how widespread they will become.
 The Turkish military reportedly plans to buy more than 500 quad-copter-type Kargu series loitering munitions,  or suicide drones, in the near term. The Kargus, at present, can operate in semi-autonomous or manually-controlled modes, but work is underway to give up to 20 of them the ability to carry out mass attacks  as a swarm, which could give Turkey's troops a potentially game-changing new capability.
 Turkey's state-run Anadolu Agency news outlet  first reported that Defense Technologies Engineering and Trade Inc., also known by its Turkish acronym  STM, was expecting to deliver the hundreds of drones to the Turkish armed forces on June 15, 2020. It's not clear if this total order for "over 500" of the drones includes or is in addition to a purchase agreement for 356 Kargus that the Turkish government  itself announced in January.
  STM introduced the first generation Kargu in 2017 and the Turkish military first began receiving small numbers of the improved Kargu-2 variant last year. Turkish forces  have reportedly at least deployed, if not employed, the drones during operations along the country's border with Syria last year.
 The manufacturer says that Kargu, a name that literally translates as "hawk" in Turkish, and which is also used to refer to small mountain watchtowers, "has been engineered specifically for anti-terror and asymmetric warfare scenarios." The 15-pound Kargu-2 can fly at up to 90 miles per hour and can remain airborne for up to 30 minutes. It has a line-of-sight control link with a range of around six miles.
  The Kargu-2 can also fly higher, has a longer range, and has the ability to remain in a designated area for a longer period of time  compared to the earlier models. It also has updated targeting capabilities and improvements to reduce its auditory signature, the latter of which helps reduce the chance that an opponent will spot the drone before it's too late.
 An operator on the ground can manually control any of the Kargu series drones and use their onboard sensors, which includes electro-optical and infrared video cameras and a laser imaging system, or LIDAR, to conduct general surveillance and identify and track targets. They can then direct the quad-copters to attack a designated threat, even if it's on the move. The loitering munitions can also safely return to their operators for re-use if no targets are found.
 The drones can carry one of three different types of warheads, including a high-explosive fragmentation one for engaging personnel and other unarmored targets in the open, a thermobaric type good for targets in confined spaces such as buildings or caves, and a shaped charge for attacking lightly armored threats. Each one weighs around three pounds. The drone's warhead can also be set to function on impact or airburst above the target, the latter being a feature particularly useful for the fragmentation and thermobaric types.
  The operator can also employ the Kargus as a traditional missile against fixed targets. In this method of attack, the drone would use its GPS navigation system to strike the desired location.
 The Kargu series of drones can also operate in a semi-autonomous mode, wherein the operator directs the quad-copter to fly to a certain area and then detect and engage targets on its own. As long as the line-of-sight control link remains unbroke, the operator remains in-the-loop throughout the process and can redirect the drone or abort its attack, if necessary.  Israel has long been a pioneer of these kinds of man-in-the-loop control systems, which are now the default for most  suicide drones and have become increasingly popular on  other types of munitions, as well.
 Most importantly, however, last year, STM announced it was working to give the Kargu family of drones additional autonomy and the ability to work together in large swarms. The swarming technology is in development as part of a larger Turkish government program  known as Kerkes, which is also looking to develop systems to improve the ability of drones to operate in GPS-denied environments, something that is increasingly a very real threat.
   It's not clear how heavily networked together the company is expecting to make the armed quad-copters, but even being able to launch  more rudimentary massed attacks with up to 20 of them at a time would offer a significant boost in capability.
 Tests have shown that a single Kargu with the air-bursting high-explosive fragmentation warhead can effectively engage clusters of personnel within a circle around 20 feet in diameter. More than one working together could evenly engage threats across a relatively wide area. Beyond just being devastating to concentrations of personnel, this could enable quick large scale attacks against other soft targets, including convoys of light vehicles, parked aircraft, radar dishes and sensor systems, ammunition and fuel dumps, and much more. With a mixture of the different warhead options presently available for Kargu, a group of the drones might be able to carry out more complex attacks, as well.
 If the swarming Kargus have the ability to operate in a fully-autonomous mode, within pre-set parameters, they could become even more capable. Swarms by their very nature can confuse and overwhelm an opponent's defenses, even those belonging to major militaries, causing havoc even if a significant number of them get shot down before they can reach their targets. This is  a very real threat that  War Zone has explored in detail on  multiple occasions in the past.
 Based on its general size and configuration, the Kargus appear to be a relatively low-cost option for providing this capability, which also underscores how low the barrier to entry for this kind of swarming technology is becoming. Beyond more robust military developments with regards to swarming, small quad-copter-type drones flying in large coordinated formations have been  employed in the commercial sector for  years now, as well.
 The swarming technology STM is developing may also be applicable to other drones and loitering munitions it is developing now. Among its other products, the company also offers a fixed-wing tube-launched loitering munition, called  Alpagu, which is very similar in form and function to  AeroVironment's Switchblade and is now also in Turkish service.
  It seems very possible that, in addition to providing these improved Kargus to the Turkish armed forces, STM could also  seek to export them, proliferating this capability further around the world. STM has already said that it has received serious inquires about the Kargu series from at least  three unnamed potential foreign customers. Turkey, as a whole, has become a  powerhouse of drone development and production, employing larger types to great effect in  Syria and  Libya just this year.
 This is precisely the type of weapon we have  been warning about for years now. The fact that it is already here and potentially exportable should be yet another wake-up call to the level of threat low-end drones pose to U.S. and allied forces, as well as domestic infrastructure  and VIPs.
 "I argue all the time with my Air Force friends that the future of flight is vertical and it's unmanned," U.S. Marine General Kenneth McKenzie, head of U.S. Central Command, said at an event hosted by the Middle East Institute  last week. "I'm not talking about large unmanned platforms, which are the size of a conventional fighter jet that we can see and deal with, as we would any other platform."
 "I'm talking about the one you can go out and buy at Costco right now in the United States for a thousand dollars, four quad, rotorcraft or something like that that can be launched and flown," he continued. "And with very simple modifications, it can make made into something that can drop a weapon like a  hand grenade or  something else."
 The Kargus, and their future swarming capabilities, could very well become the next major Turkish drone success story, for better or worse.
title: 土耳其现在拥有成群结队的自杀式无人机，可以出口
title_en: Turkey Now Has Swarming Suicide Drones It Could Export

note:
我们政府的新冠肺炎缓解政策，广泛的社会封锁，重点是不惜一切代价遏制疾病的传播，而不是“拉平曲线”，防止医院过度拥挤。尽管用心良苦，但封锁是在没有考虑其直接后果的情况下实施的，而不仅仅是大流行造成的后果。
这些政策造成了历史上最严重的全球经济混乱，造成了数万亿美元的经济产出损失。这些财务损失被错误地描绘成纯粹的经济损失。相反，我们利用美国国立卫生研究院(National Institutes Of Health)的众多公共获取出版物、疾病控制和预防中心(CDC)和劳工统计局(Bureau Of Labor Statistics)的数据，以及各种精算表，计算出这些政策将造成毁灭性的非经济后果，这将使美国累计损失数百万年的生命，远远超过病毒本身造成的损失。
纵观历史，流行病一直困扰着人类。它们摧毁了罗马帝国和拜占庭帝国、中世纪的欧洲、中国和印度，尽管医学取得了进步，但它们仍然延续到今天。
在过去的一个世纪里，见证了三次大流行，美国至少有10万人死亡：西班牙流感，1918-1919年，全球死亡人数在2000万到5000万人之间，其中包括美国的67.5万人；亚洲流感，1957-1958年，全球约有110万人死亡，其中11.6万人在美国；香港流感，1957-1958年，全球约有110万人死亡，其中11.6万人在美国；1968-1972年，全球约有100万人口，其中包括美国的10万人。到目前为止，目前的大流行已导致近10万人在美国死亡，但几乎完全的经济停摆的反应是史无前例的。
据估计，仅美国一国的经济产出损失就占GDP的5%左右，或者说每个月经济停摆的1.1万亿美元。这种收入损失导致生命损失，因为失业和提供基本需求的压力增加了自杀、酗酒或吸毒以及压力导致的疾病的发生率。这些影响对低收入人群尤其严重，因为他们更有可能失业，低收入个人的死亡率要高得多。
据统计，美国收入每损失1,000万至2,400万美元，就会额外导致一人死亡，而这种影响的一部分是通过失业造成的，这导致死亡率平均增加至少60%。这意味着，在大约3600万新失业的美国人中，每月有7200人失去生命，其中超过40%的人预计无法重新找到工作。此外，许多小企业主正面临财务崩溃，造成财富损失，导致死亡率增加50%。如果平均估计每1700万美元的收入损失中就有一人失去生命，这相当于美国每个月都会因为经济停摆而失去6.5万人的生命。
除了因收入损失而失去的生命外，由于政府停摆及其在患者中造成的恐惧，延误或放弃的医疗保健也造成了生命损失。从与神经外科同事的个人沟通来看，大约一半的患者没有出现在治疗疾病的过程中，如果不治疗，这些疾病可能会导致脑出血、瘫痪或死亡。
以下是我们计算时所依据的错过医疗保健的例子：急诊、中风和评估下降了40%。在美国接受化疗的65万名癌症患者中，估计有一半错过了最新的治疗。在美国通常每月发现的15万例新癌症病例中，大多数--就像世界其他地方一样--没有得到诊断，三分之二到四分之三的常规癌症筛查没有发生，原因是政府关门政策和民众的恐惧。与去年同期相比，现在发生的活体捐赠者移植减少了近85%。此外，超过一半的儿童和儿童疫苗接种没有进行，这可能会引发一场大规模的未来健康灾难。
治疗延误对新冠肺炎以外的情况的影响是，每月导致美国8,000人死亡，或者说剩余生命约为120,000年。错过中风可能会导致每月额外损失10万年的生命；晚期癌症诊断可能会导致每月额外损失25万年的剩余生命；错过活体捐赠者移植，每月会再损失5000年的生命-而且，如果不接种疫苗，即使只有10%的疫苗没有接种，结果也是每月额外损失2.4万年的生命。
这些因错过医疗保健而造成的意外后果相当于每月损失50多万年的寿命，这还不包括所有其他已知的跳过医疗保健。
如果我们只考虑经济停摆造成的与失业相关的死亡人数，那么每月至少会额外增加7200人的生命。假设这些死亡按比例发生在当前美国死亡数据的各个年龄段，男性和女性比例相等，这相当于经济停摆每个月损失的寿命超过20万年。
相比之下，新冠肺炎的死亡不成比例地落在了老年人身上，特别是在养老院，以及那些患有合并症的人。根据这些新冠肺炎患者的预期剩余寿命，考虑到40%的死亡发生在养老院，到目前为止，这种疾病已经造成了80万年的寿命损失。如果只考虑到仅仅因为封锁政策而错过的医疗保健和失业造成的生命损失，我们保守地估计，全国的封锁每月至少要对70万年的寿命损失负责，约合150万年。
对抗新冠肺炎影响的政策制定者必须认识到并考虑到他们的决定的全部影响。他们需要意识到关闭经济的重要部分造成的生命损失的破坏性影响。政策领导人迟来的承认封锁造成的不可弥补的伤害是远远不够的。他们需要强调和广泛地告知公众这些严重的社会后果，并通过强有力地阐明重新开放社会的理由来安抚他们对全人类生命的担忧。
为了结束经济封锁造成的生命损失，企业以及K-12学校、公共交通、公园和海滩应该聪明地重新开放，加强卫生，并对高危人群中的任何人发出基于科学的保护警告。*对于美国大部分地区来说，现在就应该重新开放，而不是任何不必要的、基于恐惧的限制，其中许多限制重复了无视证据的错误。通过遵循一项深思熟虑的分析，最终认识到所有可用的行动及其后果，我们可以拯救数百万年的美国人的生命。
当下一次大流行不可避免地出现时，我们需要记住这些教训，并遵循从一开始就考虑到所有美国人生命的政策。
斯科特·W·阿特拉斯是斯坦福大学胡佛研究所的内科医生和高级研究员。在推特上关注他，邮箱是@SWAtlasHoover。
约翰·R·伯奇(John R.Birge)是芝加哥大学布斯商学院(University Of Chicago Booth School Of Business)教授。
拉尔夫·L·基尼(Ralph L Keeney)是杜克大学(Duke University)商学和南加州大学(University Of Southern California)工程学荣誉退休教授。
亚历山大·利普顿(Alexander Lipton)是耶路撒冷希伯来大学耶路撒冷商学院的客座教授和院长研究员。
note_en:
Our governmental COVID-19 mitigation policy of broad societal lockdown focuses on containing the spread of the disease at all costs, instead of “flattening the curve” and preventing hospital overcrowding. Although well-intentioned, the lockdown was imposed without consideration of its consequences beyond those directly from the pandemic.
 The policies have created the greatest global economic disruption in history, with  trillions of dollars of lost economic output. These financial losses have been falsely portrayed as purely economic. To the contrary, using numerous National Institutes of Health Public Access publications, Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and Bureau of Labor Statistics data, and various actuarial tables, we calculate that these policies will cause devastating non-economic consequences that will total millions of accumulated years of life lost in the United States, far beyond what the virus itself has caused.
 Pandemics have afflicted humankind throughout history. They devastated the Roman and Byzantine empires, Medieval Europe, China and India, and they continue to the present day despite medical progress.
  The past century has witnessed  three pandemics with at least 100,000 U.S. fatalities: The "Spanish Flu," 1918-1919, with between 20 million and 50 million fatalities worldwide, including 675,000 in the U.S.; the "Asian Flu," 1957-1958, with about 1.1 million deaths worldwide, 116,000 of those in the U.S.; and the "Hong Kong Flu," 1968-1972,  with about 1 million people worldwide, including 100,000 in the U.S. So far, the current pandemic has produced almost 100,000 U.S. deaths, but the reaction of a near-complete economic shutdown is unprecedented.
 The lost economic output in the U.S. alone is estimated to be  5 percent of GDP, or $1.1 trillion for every month of the economic shutdown. This lost income results in lost lives as the stresses of  unemployment  and providing basic needs increase the incidence of  suicide, alcohol or drug abuse, and stress-induced illnesses. These effects are particularly severe on the lower-income populace, as they are more likely to lose their jobs, and mortality rates are much higher for lower-income individuals.
 Statistically, every $10 million to $24 million lost in U.S. incomes results in one additional  death. One portion of this effect is through unemployment, which leads to an average increase in mortality of at least 60 percent. That translates into 7,200 lives lost per month among the  36 million newly unemployed Americans, over 40 percent of whom are  not expected to regain their jobs. In addition, many small business owners are  near financial collapse, creating lost wealth that results in  mortality increases of 50 percent. With an average estimate of one additional lost life per $17 million income loss, that would translate to 65,000 lives lost in the U.S. for each month because of the economic shutdown.
 In addition to lives lost because of lost income, lives also are lost due to delayed or foregone health care imposed by the shutdown and the fear it creates among patients. From personal communications with neurosurgery colleagues, about half of their patients have not appeared for treatment of disease which, left untreated, risks brain hemorrhage, paralysis or death.
 Here are the examples of missed health care on which we base our calculations: Emergency  stroke evaluations are down 40 percent. Of the  650,000 cancer patients receiving chemotherapy in the United States, an estimated half are missing their  treatments. Of the  150,000 new cancer cases typically discovered each month in the U.S., most – as elsewhere in the world –  are not being diagnosed, and two-thirds to three-fourths of routine cancer  screenings are not happening because of shutdown policies and fear among the population. Nearly  85 percent fewer living-donor transplants are occurring now, compared to the same period last year. In addition, more than half of childhood  vaccinations are not being performed, setting up the potential of a massive future health disaster.
  The implications of  treatment delays for situations other than COVID-19 result in 8,000 U.S. deaths per month of the shutdown, or about 120,000 years of remaining life. Missed  strokes contribute an additional loss of 100,000 years of life for each month;  late cancer diagnoses lose 250,000 years of remaining life for each month; missing  living-donor transplants, another 5,000 years of life per month — and, if even 10 percent of  vaccinations are not done, the result is an additional 24,000 years of life lost each month.
 These unintended consequences of missed health care amount to more than 500,000 lost years of life per month, not including all the other known skipped care.
 If we only consider unemployment-related fatalities from the economic shutdown, that would total at least an additional 7,200 lives per month. Assuming these deaths occur proportionally across the ages of current U.S. mortality data, and equally among men and women, this amounts to more than 200,000 lost years of life for each month of the economic shutdown.
 In comparison, COVID-19 fatalities have fallen disproportionately on the elderly, particularly in  nursing homes, and those with co-morbidities. Based on the expected remaining lifetimes of these COVID-19 patients, and given that 40 percent of deaths are in nursing homes, the disease has been responsible for 800,000 lost years of life so far. Considering only the losses of life from missed health care and unemployment due solely to the lockdown policy, we conservatively estimate that the national lockdown is responsible for at least 700,000 lost years of life every month, or about 1.5 million so far — already far surpassing the COVID-19 total.
 Policymakers combatting the effects of COVID-19 must recognize and consider the full impact of their decisions. They need to be aware of the devastating effects in terms of lost life from shutting down significant parts of the economy. The belated acknowledgement by policy leaders of irreparable harms from the lockdown is not nearly enough. They need to emphatically and widely inform the public of these serious consequences and reassure them of their concern for all human life by strongly articulating the rationale for reopening society.
  To end the loss of life from the economic lockdown, businesses as well as K-12 schools, public transportation, parks and beaches should smartly reopen with enhanced hygiene and science-based protection warnings for any in the high-risk population. For most of the country, that reopening should occur now, without any unnecessary fear-based restrictions, many of which repeat the error of disregarding the evidence. By following a thoughtful analysis that finally recognizes all available actions and their consequences, we can save millions of years of American life.
 When the next pandemic inevitably arises, we need to remember these lessons and follow policies that consider the lives of all Americans from the outset.
 Scott W. Atlas is a physician and senior fellow at Stanford University’s Hoover Institution. Follow him on Twitter  @SWAtlasHoover.
  John R. Birge is a professor at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business.
 Ralph L Keeney is professor emeritus in business at Duke University and in engineering at the University of Southern California.
 Alexander Lipton is a visiting professor and dean's fellow at the Jerusalem Business School of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.
title: 新冠肺炎的停摆将使美国人失去数百万年的生命
title_en: The Covid-19 shutdown will cost Americans millions of years of life

note:
埃隆·马斯克(Elon Musk)和史蒂夫·乔布斯(Steve Jobs)被誉为商界有史以来最聪明的两个人，爱他们也好，恨他们也罢。
然而，尽管他们有处理信息、批判性思维和识别关键见解的能力--其中许多见解帮助特斯拉和苹果的估值达到数十亿美元--但两人都有一个意想不到的习惯：
尴尬沉默的规则很简单：当面对一个具有挑战性的问题时，你会停下来，深入思考你想要如何回答，而不是回答。
但别搞错了，这不是短暂的停顿。你可能需要5秒、10秒甚至15秒才能做出回应。如果你不习惯这样做，一开始会觉得很尴尬。
加勒特·赖斯曼(Garrett Reisman)是一名工程师和前宇航员，他离开NASA加入SpaceX，在最近的一次采访中描述了马斯克是如何使用这项技术的。
赖斯曼说，如果你向[埃隆]提出一个严肃的问题，他会考虑的。他会进入这个状态，几乎就像是在发呆--他会凝视着天空，你可以看到轮子在转动。他把他所有的智力都集中在这个问题上，这是相当可观的。
事实上，几乎在马斯克本人接受采访的时候，你都可以看到这种情况发生。事实上，对于马斯克来说，在给出答案之前花5到15秒的时间思考并不少见。(只要看看这次面试的20点大关就知道了。)。
众所周知，苹果联合创始人乔布斯也做了同样的事情。事实上，有一个20多年前的视频捕捉到了一个完美的例子。
那是1997年，乔布斯在十多年前被赶出公司后刚刚回到苹果。他在苹果公司的全球开发者大会(Worldwide Developers Conference)上进行问答时，一名听众对他进行了抨击，告诉乔布斯，你不知道你在说什么，还讽刺地问这位著名的创始人过去7年一直在做什么。
在乔布斯反思这个问题并仔细考虑批评之后，在相当于20秒的尴尬沉默之后，接下来的事情更是一个如何应对侮辱的高超演示。(我在这里分析了乔布斯的整个回应，如果你感兴趣的话。)。
正如这些例子所表明的那样，尴尬的沉默规则是批判性思维的一个很好的工具。它可以帮助你给出更深入、更具分析性、更深思熟虑的答案。它可以帮助你更有效地找到问题的根源，从而更好地理解问题。
但尴尬沉默的规则提供了另一个主要优势，这与我们大脑处理情绪的方式有很大关系。
情商是一种识别、理解和管理情绪的能力--尴尬的沉默规则就是一种很好的表现方式。
当谈到冷静和理性思考时，我们通常会参与大脑中被称为前额叶皮质的一部分。但是，当我们感到受到攻击或受到压力时，我们就会激活大脑的另一部分，即杏仁核，它往往会以一种情感劫持的形式接管工作。
这并不总是坏事，因为我们的情绪可以帮助我们走出困境。
当这些情绪失控，我们说了或做了一些事后后悔的事情时，问题就来了。
回想一下史蒂夫·乔布斯(Steve Jobs)回应侮辱的例子。这是乔布斯重新加入苹果后的首批重要露面之一。到他多年前离开时，他已经建立了傲慢的名声，不能很好地与他人合作。如果反应错误，他可能会在扭亏为盈计划开始之前失去公司、投资者和公众的信心。
相反，通过接受尴尬沉默的规则，他能够控制自己的情绪，并做出完美的回应。
在回答具有挑战性的问题时，你可能会忍不住想吐出任何东西，即使这没有多大意义。或者，你可能会说你认为别人想听的话，而不是你真正相信的。
但这真的是你想要的吗？或者你更愿意花几秒钟的时间停下来，把事情想清楚，然后再以一种你引以为豪的方式回应呢？
因此，下次有人问你一个具有挑战性的问题时，试着在给出答案之前慢慢来。在这样做的过程中，您将：
你练习得越多，尴尬的停顿就不再让你感到尴尬--而且你越能让情绪对你起作用，而不是对你不利。
note_en:
Love them or hate them,  Elon Musk and  Steve Jobs are known as two of the most brilliant minds the business world has ever seen.
 Yet, despite their ability to process information, think critically, and identify key insights--many of which helped Tesla and Apple reach valuations into the billions of dollars--both men practice(d) an unexpected habit:
  The rule of awkward silence is simple: When faced with a challenging question, instead of answering, you pause and think deeply about how you want to answer.
  But make no mistake, this is no short pause. You might go five, 10, or even 15 seconds before offering a response. Which, if you're not used to doing it, will feel very awkward--at first.
 Garrett Reisman, an engineer and former astronaut who left NASA to join SpaceX, described how Musk uses this technique in  a recent interview.
 "If you pose to [Elon] a serious question," says Reisman, "he'll consider it. And he'll kind of go into this, almost like a trance--he'll stare off into space and you can see the wheels turning. And he's focusing all of his intellect, which is considerable, on this one question."
 You can actually see this happen almost anytime Musk himself gives an interview. In fact, it's not uncommon for Musk to take from between five to even 15 seconds to think before giving an answer. (Just check out the  20:00 mark of this interview.)
 Apple co-founder Jobs was known to do the same. In fact, there's a perfect example  captured on video over 20 years ago.
 It was 1997, and Jobs had just returned to Apple after being ousted from the company over a decade earlier. He was conducting a Q&A at the company's Worldwide Developers Conference when an audience member took a shot at him, telling Jobs, "You don't know what you're talking about" and sarcastically asking what the famous founder had been working on for the past seven years.
       What follows, after Jobs reflects on the question and contemplates the criticism, in what amounts to 20 seconds of awkward silence, is a masterful demonstration of how to respond to an insult. (I  broke down Jobs's entire response here, if you're interested.)
 As these examples illustrate, the rule of awkward silence is a great tool of critical thinking. It can help you to give deeper, more analytical, more thoughtful answers. It can help you get to root problems more effectively, which leads to greater understanding.
 But the rule of awkward silence offers another major advantage, and it has much to do with the way our brains process emotions.
  Emotional intelligence is the ability to identify, understand, and manage emotions--and the rule of awkward silence is a great way to show it.
 When it comes to calm and rational thought, we typically engage a part of our brain known as the prefrontal cortex. But when we feel attacked or under pressure, we engage another part of our brain known as the amygdala, which tends to take over in a type of "emotional hijack."
 That's not always bad, as our emotions can help us get out of difficult situations.
 The problem comes when those emotions go unchecked, and we say or do things that we later regret.
 Think back to the example of Steve Jobs responding to the insult. This was one of Jobs's first major appearances after rejoining Apple. By the time he left many years previous, he had built a reputation as being arrogant and unable to work well with others. With the wrong response, he could have lost the confidence of his company, investors, and the public before his turnaround plans got underway.
 Instead, by embracing the rule of awkward silence, he was able to keep his emotions under control and deliver a perfect response.
  When it comes to answering challenging questions, you might be tempted to just spit out anything, even if doesn't make much sense. Or, you might say what you think the other person wants to hear instead of what you truly believe.
 But is that what you really want? Or would you prefer to take some seconds to pause, to think things through, and then to respond in a way you're proud of later?
 So, the next time someone asks you a challenging question, try to take your time before giving an answer. In doing so, you will:
  The more you practice, the awkward pause won't feel so awkward anymore--and the more you will be able to make emotions work for you, instead of against you.

路透法兰克福/马尼拉8月23日电-在菲律宾搜寻陷入困境的电子支付公司Wirecard失踪的21亿美元现金后，Wirecard首席执行官周五辞职.。
马库斯·布劳恩(Markus Braun)将这家德国公司打造成欧洲最热门的投资之一，他让Wirecard面临着迫在眉睫的现金紧缩，原因是有人指控他在丢失资金方面存在欺诈行为。
该公司在一份声明中表示，德国证券交易所前合规官James Freis已被任命为临时首席执行官。
三名知情人士表示，Wirecard正在与银行举行紧急谈判，以确保财务生命线，此前其审计师安永(EY)拒绝在其账户上签字。
该公司周四警告称，如果周五不公布年报，大约20亿欧元(22.4亿美元)的贷款可能会被终止。
在两家菲律宾银行表示这家德国支付公司不是他们的客户，并声称文件被伪造后，该公司的股价再次暴跌。
布劳恩一直在积极为该公司辩护，反对会计欺诈的指控，他早些时候曾表示，该公司本身可能是欺诈的受害者。
布劳恩没有确认他涉嫌欺诈的人，而两家菲律宾银行BPI和BDO都发表了声明，否认与Wirecard有任何关系。
“Wirecard不是该银行的客户。声称在BDO开设Wirecard户口的文件是伪造文件，并附有伪造的银行职员签名。“。
BDO在一份声明中补充说：“此事已经报告给菲律宾邦科哨兵队(Bangko Sentral Ng Pilipinas)。”他指的是菲律宾央行。
“他们的外部审计师向我们提交了一份文件，声称他们是客户。我们已经确定这份文件是伪造的。我们将继续调查此事，“BPI在一份声明中称。
近年来，安永(EY)定期批准Wirecard的账户，该公司拒绝在2019年签字，证实了毕马威(KPMG)4月份在一项外部调查中发现的缺陷。
Wirecard在德国是一个受欢迎的技术成功故事，在重工业中声名鹊起。但在一名举报人声称它的成功部分归功于虚假交易网络后，它的命运瓦解了。
布劳恩面临辞职的呼声，但他和公司驳斥了这些指控，以及本周未能从审计师那里赢得一份干净的健康证明，粉碎了投资者的信心。

FRANKFURT/MANILA (Reuters) - The chief executive of Wirecard resigned on Friday after the search for $2.1 billion of cash missing from the embattled electronic payments firm hit a dead end in the Philippines.
 Markus Braun, who built the German company into one of the hottest investments in Europe, leaves Wirecard facing a looming cash crunch amid allegations of fraud over the missing money.
 In a statement, the company said James Freis, a former compliance officer at Germany’s stock exchange, had been appointed as interim CEO.
 Wirecard is holding emergency talks with banks to secure a financial lifeline, three people with knowledge of the matter said, after its auditor, EY, refused to sign off on its accounts.
 The company warned on Thursday that loans of roughly 2 billion euros ($2.24 billion) could be terminated if its annual report is not published on Friday.
  The company’s shares tanked again after two Philippine banks said the German payments company was not a client of theirs and alleged that documents had been falsified.
  Braun, who has aggressively defended the company against accusations of accounting fraud, had earlier said that the firm could itself have been the victim of fraud.
 Braun had not identified those he suspected of fraud, while BPI and BDO, the two Philippine banks, both issued statements denying any relationship with Wirecard.
 “Wirecard is not a client of the bank. The document claiming the existence of a Wirecard account with BDO is a falsified document and carries forged signatures of bank officers,” BDO said.
 “The matter has already been reported to the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas,” BDO added in a statement, referring to the Philippines’ central bank.
  “Their external auditor presented to us a document that claimed that they are a client. We have determined that the document is spurious. We continue to investigate this matter,” BPI said in a statement.
 EY had regularly approved Wirecard’s accounts in recent years, and its refusal to sign off for 2019 confirms failings found in an external probe by KPMG in April.
  Wirecard was a welcome technology success story in Germany, which made its name in heavy industry. But its fortunes unravelled after a whistleblower alleged that it owed its success in part to a web of sham transactions.
 Braun faced calls to resign but he and the company dismissed the claims and its failure to win a clean bill of health this week from auditors for its accounts shattered investor confidence.
title: Wirecard首席执行官辞职，寻找失踪数十亿美元在亚洲陷入死胡同
title_en: Wirecard CEO quits as search for missing billions hits dead end in Asia

每天早上在你的收件箱里获取科技领域最重要的信息。注册源代码。
苹果公司告诉Basecamp团队，它有几个选择。它可以将嘿变成现有服务之外的标准电子邮件客户端，并允许用户使用现有的Gmail和其他账户登录。或者，它也可以让用户在iOS应用程序内为嘿付费。我们很乐意继续支持你的应用程序业务，并为你提供免费提供服务的解决方案。该公司写道，只要你遵循并尊重所有开发者都必须遵守的同样的App Store审查指南和条款。但它表示，如果没有任何变化，应用程序审查委员会的裁决将维持不变。
这一周围绕苹果商店政策充满了辩论，此前欧盟启动了一项反垄断调查，“嘿”的开发者指控该公司敲诈他们，以削减他们的收入。从那时起，许多开发人员分享了他们自己与App Store的复杂交互。不幸的是，发布iOS软件意味着要上iOS应用商店。正因为如此，许多开发者都不敢直言不讳，应用程序制造商Rogue Amoeba在推特上写道。这种持续沉默的可悲结果是，苹果可能会滥用他们的权力地位，他们确实是这样做的。
与此同时，反垄断指控的声音越来越大。微软总裁布拉德·史密斯(Brad Smith)告诉彭博社(Bloomberg)，监管机构应该对应用商店进行全面调查，因为他们强加的要求越来越多地表明，进入我们平台的方法只有一种，那就是穿过我们自己创建的大门。众议院反垄断委员会主席大卫·西西林(David Cicilline)告诉The Verge，苹果的收费相当于高速公路抢劫，并指控苹果欺凌和压制小开发者。
在周四发信前的一次电话中，苹果再次重申，嘿违反了规则，从一开始就不应该出现在App Store上。该公司还表示，嘿应该知道会被拒绝：它的Mac应用程序立即被拒绝，因为它违反了与iOS应用程序相同的应用内购买规则。该公司坚称，它没有改变规则，最近也没有加大对开发人员的压力--尽管开发人员表示，他们已经感受到了这一点。App Review团队会犯错误，但仅此而已。
苹果再次试图解释哪些应用可以不使用应用内购买，哪些可以不使用。首先，如果一款应用是一种专门消费在其他地方购买的内容的方式，比如歌曲、电影或播客，它们就不一定要在应用内购买。这些被称为阅读器应用程序，它们在指南中是免责的-尽管即使这样很快就会变得一团糟，因为经过批准的服务，如教室管理应用程序，也被认为是阅读器应用程序。更复杂的是，苹果的指导方针中没有这样一条规则：如果一款应用由一家公司支付费用，并由管理员管理--比如Salesforce、人力资源服务，所有普通人从来不会为之付费的东西，而且完全由IT部门运营--该应用不一定需要在应用内购买。
苹果公司表示，嘿不是这样的。嘿是一款消费者应用，由用户付费。苹果不同意这样一种观点，即当这些潜在用户之一下载这款应用时，他们无能为力-他们不能注册，他们不能支付，他们必须去其他地方才能使用这款应用。苹果不喜欢这样的应用程序，至少当它觉得这些应用程序应该更容易为目标受众所接受的时候是这样。
苹果表示，自App Store启动以来的几年里，它只是通过创建更多的豁免和简化规则，为开发者提供了更多避免应用内购买费用的方法。但它似乎永远不可能允许应用程序，比方说，收集信用卡信息或通过应用程序内的PayPal处理订阅。而且它觉得有权获得自己的收入份额，就像创建和维护开发者使用的商店的公司一样。
苹果确实考虑到了这样一个事实，即它可能需要澄清和重新考虑其指导方针，以使事情变得更加清晰。目前，Basecamp为了让其应用程序获得批准，正在加快其嘿for Business项目-首席技术官兼联合创始人大卫·海内迈尔·汉森(David Heinemeier Hansson)告诉我，该公司已经吸引了几名客户。他们希望通过将“嘿”变成公司付费的服务，他们可以改变苹果对待其应用程序的方式。我问海内梅尔·汉森(Heinemeier Hansson)他是否认为这会奏效。他回复了一条短信，编号为&#34；，然后一秒钟后：&#34；😂。&#34；
你好，杰森，我们写信是想让你知道你的应用程序的上诉结果，嘿，电子邮件。应用审核委员会对您的应用进行了评估，并确定拒绝是有效的。您的应用程序不符合下面详细说明的App Store审核准则。如你所知，这就是你的嘿电子邮件应用在2020年6月11日提交到Mac应用商店时被拒绝的原因。嘿电子邮件应用程序在App Store上作为电子邮件应用程序进行营销，但当用户下载您的应用程序时，它不起作用。用户不能使用这款应用程序访问电子邮件或执行任何有用的功能，直到他们转到Basecamp网站查看嘿电子邮件并购买了使用嘿电子邮件应用程序的许可证。这违反了以下App Store审查准则：准则3.1.1-Business-Payments-In-App Payments如果您想要解锁应用内的特性或功能，则必须使用应用内购买。您的应用程序要求客户在应用程序之外购买内容、订阅或功能，但这些项目不能作为应用程序内购买，如应用商店审核指南所要求的那样。准则3.1.3(A)-商务-支付-Reader应用程序阅读器应用程序允许用户访问以前购买的内容和内容订阅。根据本指南，您的邮件应用程序不在阅读器应用程序允许的内容类型之列(具体地说：杂志、报纸、书籍、音频、音乐、视频、访问专业数据库、VOIP、云存储或经批准的服务，如教室管理应用程序)。因此，必须允许客户选择使用应用内购买来购买您的应用中的特性或功能。准则3.1.3(B)-业务-支付-跨多个平台运营服务的多平台服务应用程序可以允许用户在其他平台或您的网站上访问他们在您的应用程序中获取的内容、订阅或功能，前提是这些项目也可以作为应用程序内购买。您的嘿电子邮件应用程序不提供访问内容、订阅或功能的权限，而不像应用程序内的应用程序购买那样提供访问权限。事实上，在用户进入Basecamp嘿电子邮件网站开始免费试用或购买单独的许可证将应用程序用于预期目的之前，这款应用程序不会作为电子邮件应用程序运行，也不会有任何用途。下一步要解决此问题，请修改您的应用程序，使其不违反任何App Store审核指南和条款。您可以通过多种方式修改您的应用程序或服务，以遵守App Store审查指南。以前在其他地方购买了内容、订阅或功能访问权限的客户可以继续访问您的应用程序中的这些项目，只要新iOS客户可以按照应用商店审核指南的要求选择使用应用内购买来购买访问权限。如果您不想为用户提供应用内购买的选项，您可以考虑让该应用程序具有市场上的功能-一个使用标准IMAP和POP电子邮件帐户的电子邮件客户端，客户可以选择将嘿电子邮件服务配置为他们首选的电子邮件服务提供商。这将允许该应用程序作为电子邮件客户端运行，而不需要额外付费才能使用其特性和功能。在这种方式下，你在你的网站上销售的东西显然是一项电子邮件服务，与你在App Store上分发的应用程序的功能是分开的。我们在这里作为一个资源，让您探索这些或其他想法，使嘿电子邮件应用程序符合App Store审查指南和条款的要求。感谢您成为iOS应用程序开发人员。我们了解到，Basecamp多年来已经为App Store开发了多个应用程序和许多后续版本，App Store已经向iOS用户分发了数百万个这样的应用程序。这些应用程序不提供应用内购买-因此，没有为应用程序S贡献任何收入

Power        Apple sent a letter to Basecamp's Jason Fried on Thursday with a simple message: We're not going to allow the Hey app into the App Store as it is. There will be no more escalations, no hope of a rogue executive changing the outcome. Unless you make changes, you're not getting your app approved.
  Get what matters in tech, in your inbox every morning. Sign up for Source Code.
 Apple told the Basecamp team it has a few options. It can turn Hey into a standard email client in addition to the existing service, and allow users to log in with their existing Gmail and other accounts. Alternatively, it can let users pay for Hey from within the iOS app. "We are happy to continue to support you in your app business and offer you the solutions to provide your services for free," the company wrote, "so long as you follow and respect the same App Store Review Guidelines and terms that all developers must follow." But if there are no changes, it says, the App Review Board's ruling stands.
  It's been a week full of debate over Apple's store policies, after the EU launched an antitrust investigation and the developers of Hey accused the company of shaking them down for a cut of their revenues. Since then, a number of developers have shared their own complicated interactions with the App Store. "Unfortunately, shipping iOS software means being on the iOS App Store. Because of this, many developers are scared to speak out," the app maker  Rogue Amoeba tweeted. "The sad result of this continued silence is that Apple can abuse their position of power, and they are."
 Meanwhile, the antitrust allegations keep getting louder. Microsoft President Brad Smith  told Bloomberg that regulators should investigate app stores in general, because "they impose requirements that increasingly say there is only one way to get on to our platform and that is to go through the gate that we ourselves have created." Rep. David Cicilline, the head of the House antitrust committee,  told The Verge that Apple's fees amount to "highway robbery" and accused Apple of bullying and crushing small developers.
 In a call ahead of sending its letter on Thursday, Apple reiterated again that Hey broke its rules and should have never made it onto the App Store in the first place. It also said Hey should have known the rejection was coming: Its Mac app was immediately rejected for violating the same in-app purchase rules as the iOS one. The company maintains it hasn't changed its rules and hasn't recently increased its squeeze on developers — though developers say they've felt it. The App Review team makes mistakes, but that's all that they are.
  And again, Apple tried to explain the rules around which apps can get away with not using in-app purchases and which can't. First, if an app is a way to exclusively consume content purchased elsewhere, like songs or movies or podcasts, they don't have to have in-app purchases. These are known as  Reader apps, and they're exempt in the guidelines — though even this gets messy quickly, as "approved services such as classroom management apps" are considered Reader apps as well. Even more complicated, and not found in Apple's guidelines, is this rule: If an app is paid for by a company and managed by an administrator — think Salesforce, HR services, all the things no regular person ever pays for and are run entirely by IT departments — that app doesn't have to require in-app purchases.
 That, Apple said, is not what Hey is. Hey is a consumer app, paid for by users. And Apple takes issue with the idea that when one of those prospective users downloads the app, there's nothing they can do with it — they can't sign up, they can't pay, they have to go somewhere else before they can use the app. Apple doesn't like apps like that, at least when it feels they should be more accessible to their intended audience.
  In the years since the App Store started, Apple said, it has only offered more ways for developers to avoid the in-app-purchase toll, by creating more exemptions and simplifying the rules. But it seems unlikely to ever allow an app to, say, collect credit card information or process subscriptions through PayPal within the app. And it feels entitled to its portion of revenues, as the company that created and maintains the store developers use.
 Apple did allow for the fact that it may need to clarify and rethink its guidelines to make things clearer. For now, Basecamp, in an effort to get its app approved, is accelerating its Hey For Business program — CTO and co-founder David Heinemeier Hansson told me the company's already onboarded a couple of customers. They're hoping that by turning Hey into a service companies pay for, they can change the way Apple treats its app. I asked Heinemeier Hansson if he thought it was going to work. He responded in a text, "NO." Then a second later: "😂."
  Hello Jason,  We are writing to let you know the appeal results for your app, HEY Email.  The App Review Board evaluated your app and determined that the rejection was valid. Your app does not comply with the  App Store Review Guidelines detailed below. As you are aware, this is the reason your Hey Email app was rejected when it was submitted to the Mac App Store on June 11, 2020.  The HEY Email app is marketed as an email app on the App Store, but when users download your app, it does not work. Users cannot use the app to access email or perform any useful function until after they go to the Basecamp website for Hey Email and purchase a license to use the HEY Email app. This violates the following App Store Review Guidelines:   Guideline 3.1.1 - Business - Payments - In-App Purchase  If you want to unlock features or functionality within your app, you must use in-app purchase. Your app requires customers to purchase content, subscriptions, or features outside of the app, but those items are not available as in-app purchases within the app as required by the App Store Review Guidelines.    Guideline 3.1.3(a) - Business - Payments - "Reader" Apps  Reader apps may allow users to access previously purchased content and content subscriptions. Your mail app is not one of the content types allowed under this guideline for "Reader" apps (specifically: magazines, newspapers, books, audio, music, video, access to professional databases, VOIP, cloud storage, or approved services such as classroom management apps). Therefore, customers must be given the option to purchase access to features or functionality in your app using in-app purchase.   Guideline 3.1.3(b) - Business - Payments - Multiplatform Services  Apps that operate services across multiple platforms may allow users to access content, subscriptions, or features they have acquired in your app on other platforms or on your website, provided those items are also available as in-app purchases within the app. Your HEY Email app does not offer access to content, subscriptions, or features as in-app purchases within the app. In fact, the app does not function as an email app or for any purpose until the user goes to the Basecamp Hey Email website to start a free trial or purchase a separate license to use the app for its intended purpose.   Next Steps  To resolve this issue, please revise your app such that it does not violate any of the App Store Review Guidelines and terms.  There are a number of ways that you could revise your app or service to adhere to the App Store Review Guidelines. Customers who have previously purchased access to content, subscriptions, or features elsewhere may continue to access these items in your app, as long as new iOS customers are given the option to purchase access using in-app purchase as required by the App Store Review Guidelines.  If you would prefer not to offer users the option of in-app purchases, you could consider having the app function as marketed — an email client that works with standard IMAP and POP email accounts, where customers can optionally configure the Hey Email service as their preferred email service provider. This would allow the app to function as an email client without requiring an additional payment to use its features and functionality. Under this approach, what you sell on your website is clearly an email service separate from the function of your app as distributed on the App Store.  We are here as a resource as you explore these or other ideas to bring the Hey Email app within compliance of the App Store Review Guidelines and terms.  Thank you for being an iOS app developer. We understand that Basecamp has developed a number of apps and many subsequent versions for the App Store for many years, and that the App Store has distributed millions of these apps to iOS users. These apps do not offer in-app purchase — and, consequently, have not contributed any revenue to the App Store over the last eight years. We are happy to continue to support you in your app business and offer you the solutions to provide your services for free — so long as you follow and respect the same App Store Review Guidelines and terms that all developers must follow.  We hope to assist you in offering the Hey Email app on the App Store.  Sincerely, App Review Board
title: 苹果致信嘿：要么换个应用，要么退出App Store
title_en: Apple sends a letter to Hey: Change your app or get out of the App Store

狗比几乎任何其他驯养的物种都更渴望人类的眼神交流。当他们在人们身边长大时，他们在四周大的时候就开始争先恐后地争取我们的注意。对大多数人来说，很难抗拒小狗眼神的任性闪现--根据一项新的研究，这种牵动心弦的眼神可能就是为什么狗能给我们那些眼神的原因。
今天发表在“美国国家科学院院刊”上的一篇论文发现，由于有一对特殊的肌肉构成了狗的眼睛，狗的脸具有狼所不具备的复杂表情结构。这些肌肉负责“领养我”的样子，狗可以通过抬起它们的内眉毛来拉动它。这是科学家发现的第一个生物学证据，驯养的狗可能进化出了一种专门用来更好地与人类沟通的能力。
在这项研究中，朴茨茅斯大学狗认知中心的一个研究小组观察了两块肌肉，这两块肌肉共同作用，可以让狗的眼睛变大、睁开，使它们看起来更大、更下垂，客观上也更可爱。眼外侧牵引肌和眼内侧提肌(幸运地称为Raol和LAOM)形成两条短而直的线，将狗眼睛周围的肌肉环连接到眉毛的两端。
长期以来，这些研究人员一直对狗与人类进行眼神接触的方式感兴趣，特别是它们如何移动眉毛。2017年，这篇新论文的主要作者朱莉安·卡明斯基(Juliane Kaminski)发现，当人类注意到狗的时候，狗移动眉毛的频率更高，而当人们忽视它们或给它们食物时，狗移动眉毛的次数更少(很遗憾，这对它们来说比人类的爱更令人兴奋)。这表明这场运动在某种程度上是自愿的。在我们这些渴望的一瞥中，研究还表明，当狗锻炼这些肌肉时，人类的反应更积极。当人和狗相互锁定时，它们都会从催产素的震动中受益。
这不仅仅是一个偶然的爱情故事，在这个故事中，两个物种的眼睛恰好在一个拥挤的星球上相遇。像所有最好的合作伙伴一样，这一次更有可能是多年进化和增长的结果。如果狗的眉毛操纵技能是因为它们与人类的联系而发展起来的，那么判断的一种方法就是在狼身上寻找同样的能力。由于狗早在33000年前就从它们的狼亲戚-特别是灰狼-中分离出来，研究这两种动物有点像打开一个四条腿的时间胶囊。这两个物种之间的差异标志着狗的驯化的开始，这是一个长期的进化过程，受到人类的影响，而且往往是直接驱动的。今天，研究人员可以识别和研究不同物种之间的差异，以确切地了解狗是如何随着时间的推移而发生变化的。
在这种情况下，那些挑起眉毛的肌肉看起来确实是对狗的解剖学的补充。在研究人员观察的四只灰狼中，两块肌肉都不存在。(他们确实发现了纤维束，可能是RAOL和LAOM的前驱物质。)。在研究人员观察的六个品种的狗中，有五个品种的两块肌肉都完全形成并强壮；而在西伯利亚哈士奇犬中，研究人员找不到Raol犬。西伯利亚哈士奇是最古老的狼形品种。
有时，这些变化的起因并不是一目了然的。某些狗的生理特征--包括松弛的耳朵和短小的鼻子--很可能来自同一组发育细胞，这些细胞编码温顺，例如，这是家养宠物更喜欢的特征。然而，在这项新的研究中，身体特征和相关行为之间的联系更为直接。杜克大学(Duke University)犬类认知中心主任、该论文编辑布莱恩·黑尔(Brian Hare)通过电子邮件告诉“大西洋月刊”(The Atlantic)，“之前的研究--以及这些作者的大部分研究--已经表明，这些肌肉负责增强人类的积极反应，但目前的研究表明，这些面部表情是在狗和狼分开后产生的。”
按照进化标准，从分裂开始，两块新的面部肌肉发育的时间非常短。对于一个物种来说，变化如此之快，一定是有一种相当强大的力量在作用着它。这就是人类的用武之地。我们与能够夸大眼睛大小和宽度的动物有着深刻的联系，这使得它们看起来像我们自己的人类婴儿，并“劫持”了我们的养育本能。研究已经证明，人类更喜欢面部特征更像婴儿的宠物，两年前，这项最新研究的作者表明，通过Raol和LAOM肌肉实现面部运动的狗--我们认为这是一种明显的人类表情--比那些没有这样做的狗更有可能被从收容所收养。我们可能没有培育出具有这种特征的狗，因为我们知道
波士顿学院(Boston College)研究犬类认知的助理教授安吉·约翰斯顿(Angie Johnston)没有参与这项研究，她说：“我们拥有这样一个经典的人类系统，我们与自己的婴儿互动的方式。”“在狗的认知和观察狗的驯化过程中，一次又一次出现的一个大主题是，在很多方面，它们似乎真的以婴儿或小孩子的角色直接进入了我们的社会。”他说，“在很多方面，狗的认知和对狗的驯化都是一个大主题，它们似乎真的是以婴儿或小孩子的角色直接进入了我们的社会。他们在利用我们现有的系统。“。
同样像人类的面部手势也可能是狗首先简单地获得注意力的方式。扬起眉毛是研究人员所称的明示暗示最广为人知的例子之一，明示暗示是人类相互发送的一系列非语言信号(通常是面部动作和表情)，以表达他们直接交流的意图。狗模仿人类表情的神奇能力可能会让我们将人类的某些情感投射到它们身上，这是我们在其他动物身上没有的，不管它们实际上可能会有什么感觉。
RAOL和LAOM肌肉的运动特别容易被解读。巴纳德学院狗认知实验室的高级研究员亚历山德拉·霍洛维茨(Alexandra Horowitz)说：“在不同的情况下，我们会称之为不同的东西。”“在一种情况下，我可能会说这很可悲，但在另一种情况下，我会说，他真的很注意。它可能看起来很扭曲，就像是一种质疑或不相信的表情。“。根据霍洛维茨的说法，除了我们的灵长类表亲之外，狗是唯一一种以这种令人毛骨悚然的熟悉方式表达的动物。只有马有同样的能力将眼睛扭曲成同样悲伤的形状，但它们的整体表情并不像狗那样给我们留下像人类一样的印象。霍洛维茨指出，对于狗，我们是如此强烈地想要建立联系，以至于我们经常搜索狗嘴巴形状的“微笑”。她说，这项新的研究“让我认为更多的是能够以人类移动面部的方式移动面部。”我们不喜欢没有表情的面孔。“。
霍洛维茨和约翰斯顿都认为，对野狗(约翰斯顿研究的)和西伯利亚狐狸种群的类似研究可以为理解眉毛运动和其他进化特征提供另一种时间胶囊。这两个物种都生活在人类附近，都是最早的狗的近亲。为什么在狗被驯化的时候，它们还保持野生状态？霍洛维茨说：“任何与弄清我们作为一个物种为什么选择这种动物的原因有关的事情都可以携带大量的信息。”“在某些方面，它是在发现我们自己的一些东西。”

note_en:
Dogs, more so than almost any other domesticated species, are desperate for human eye contact. When raised around people, they begin fighting for our attention when they’re as young as four weeks old. It’s hard for most people to resist a petulant flash of puppy-dog eyes—and according to a new study, that pull on the heartstrings might be exactly why dogs can give us those looks at all.
   A paper published today in the  Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences found that dogs’ faces are  structured for complex expression in a way that wolves’ aren’t, thanks to a special pair of muscles framing their eyes. These muscles are responsible for that “adopt me” look that dogs can pull by raising their inner eyebrows. It’s the first biological evidence scientists have found that domesticated dogs might have evolved a specialized ability used expressly to communicate better with humans.
 For the study, a team at the University of Portsmouth’s Dog Cognition Centre looked at two muscles that work together to widen and open a dog’s eyes, causing them to appear bigger, droopier, and objectively cuter. The retractor anguli oculi lateralis muscle and the levator anguli oculi medialis muscle (mercifully known as RAOL and LAOM) form two short, straight lines, which connect the ring of muscle around a dog’s eye to either end of the brow above.
    These researchers have long been interested in the ways dogs make eye contact with humans and, in particular, how they move their eyebrows. In 2017, Juliane Kaminski, the lead author of the new paper, found that dogs moved their eyebrows more often while a human paid attention to them, and less often when they were ignored or given food (which, sorry to say, is a more exciting stimulus for them than human love). That suggested the movement is to some degree voluntary. On our side of these longing glances, research has also shown that when dogs work these muscles, humans respond more positively. And both man and mutt benefit from a jolt of oxytocin when locked in on each other.
  This isn’t simply a fortuitous love story, in which the eyes of two species just so happen to meet across a crowded planet. Like all the best partnerships, this one is more likely the result of years of evolution and growth. If dogs developed their skill for eyebrow manipulation because of their connection to humans, one way to tell would be to look for the same capacity in wolves. Because dogs split off from their wolf relatives—specifically, gray wolves—as many as 33,000 years ago, studying the two animals is a bit like cracking open a four-legged time capsule. Divergence between the two species marked the start of dogs’ domestication, a long evolutionary process influenced—and often directly driven—by humans. Today, researchers can identify and study differences between the species to gain an understanding of exactly how dogs have changed over time.
  In this case, those eyebrow-raising muscles do appear to be an addition to dogs’ anatomy. In the four gray wolves the researchers looked at, neither muscle was present. (They did find bundles of fibers that could be the precursors to the RAOL and LAOM.) In five of the six breeds of dogs the researchers looked at, both muscles were fully formed and strong; in the Siberian husky, the wolflike, oldest breed of the group, the researchers were unable to locate a RAOL.
 Sometimes, the origins of changes like these aren’t immediately apparent. Certain physical dog traits—including floppy ears and short snouts—likely originate from the same set of  developmental cells that code for tameness, a preferable trait in household pets, for instance. In the case of this new research, though, the connection between the physical trait and the related behavior is a bit more direct. “Previous work—and much of it by these same authors—had shown that these muscles were responsible for enhancing positive responses in humans,” Brian Hare, the director of Duke University’s Canine Cognition Center and the editor of the paper, told  The Atlantic via email, “but the current suggests the origin of these facial expressions is after dogs split from wolves.”
    By evolutionary standards, the time since this split has been remarkably short for two new facial muscles to have developed. For a species to change that quickly, a pretty powerful force must be acting on it. And that’s where humans come in. We connect profoundly with animals capable of exaggerating the size and width of their eyes, which makes them look like our own human babies and “ hijacks” our nurturing instincts. Research has already demonstrated that humans prefer pets with more  infantlike facial features, and two years ago, the authors of this latest study showed that dogs who made the facial movement enabled by the RAOL and LAOM muscles—an expression we read as distinctly humanlike—were more likely to be selected for adoption from a shelter than those who didn’t. We might not have bred dogs for this trait knowingly, but they gained so much from having it that it became a widespread facial feature. “These muscles evolved during domestication, but almost certainly due to an advantage they gave dogs during interactions with humans that we humans have been all but unaware of,” Hare explained.
  “It’s such a classically human system that we have, the ways we interact with our own infants,” says Angie Johnston, an assistant professor at Boston College who studies canine cognition and was not involved with the study. “A big theme that’s come out again and again in canine cognition and looking at the domestication of dogs is that it seems like they really just kind of dove right into our society in the role of being an infant or a small child in a lot of ways. They’re co-opting existing systems we have.”
   The same humanlike facial gestures could also be a dog’s way of simply securing attention in the first place. Eyebrow raising is one of the most well-understood examples of what researchers call ostensive cues, a family of nonverbal signals (often facial movements and expressions) humans send one another to convey their intention to directly communicate. Dogs’ uncanny ability to mimic this human expression likely leads us to project certain human emotions onto them in ways we don’t with other animals, regardless of what they might actually be feeling.
 The movement of the RAOL and LAOM muscles is particularly open to interpretation. “In different contexts we’ll call that something different,” says Alexandra Horowitz, a senior research fellow at the Barnard College Dog Cognition Lab. “In one case, I might say it’s sad, but in another case I’ll say,  He’s really paying attention. It can look wry, like a questioning or unbelieving look.” According to Horowitz, dogs are the only animals aside from our primate cousins that are expressive in this eerily familiar way. Horses alone share the ability to twist their eyes into the same doleful shape, but their overall expressions don’t strike us as humanlike in the same way that dogs’ do. With dogs, Horowitz points out, we’re so driven to connect that we often search for “smiles” in the shapes of  dogs’ mouths. The new research, she says, “makes me think it’s more about being able to move the face in a way that humans move the face. We don’t like unexpressive faces.”
    Both Horowitz and Johnston suggested that similar studies looking at populations of dingoes ( which Johnston researches) and Siberian foxes could provide yet another time capsule of sorts for understanding eyebrow movements and other evolutionary traits. Both species live near humans and are some of the closest living relatives to the earliest dogs. Why did they stay wild while dogs drifted into domestication? “Anything to do with getting to the bottom of why we as a species picked out this one animal can carry a huge amount of information,” Horowitz says. “In some ways, it’s discovering something about ourselves.”

本文是介绍基本机器学习算法的系列文章的第一篇。每个帖子将被分成两部分。
理念和关键概念-大多数人应该能够阅读本节内容并了解算法是如何工作的🤞。
细节-这是为感兴趣的读者准备的，将包括详细的数学推导，然后用Python🐍实现。
回归是任何接受输入集合并预测输出的算法。如果你还是不跟我走，我会原谅你的。
让我们举个例子，这应该更有意义。比方说，我们正在试图预测🏡在伦敦北部理想地区的一套房子会卖到多少钱。我们可能知道一些关于房子的事实，例如房子🏠的平方英尺，花园🌳的平方英尺，以及是否有车库🚙。
在机器学习中，我们将这些事实称为变量或特征。直观地说，我们可以使用这些特征的组合来评估房子的价值。🏠🌳🚙。
线性回归使用特征的线性组合来预测输出。这仅仅意味着将每个特征值乘以一个数字(称为系数)来表示该特征的重要性，例如。
$$\BEGIN{ALIGNED}\TEXT{房价}&ANP；=(GB1000*\TEXT{🏠})\\&ANP；+(GB 50*\TEXT{🌳})\\&ANP；+(GB1000*\TEXT{🚙？})\END{ALIGNED}$$。
在这个例子中，房价的计算方法是房子每平方英尺1000 GB$，加上花园里每英尺50 GB$，如果有车库的话再加1000美元。如果我们选择一处600美元一平方英尺的漂亮房产，带一个500美元一平方英尺的小花园，没有车库，我们的线性回归模型会预测这所房子的价值是625,000美元。
$$\开始{对齐}\文本{房价}&amp；=(GB 1000*\文本{🏠})+(GB 50*\文本{🌳})+(GB 1000*\文本{🚙？})\\&amp；=(GB1000*600)+(GB 50*500)+(GB1000*0)\\&amp；=GB 625,000\结束{对齐}$$。
这就是线性回归模型的工作原理！现在的问题是如何选择要素的系数，在我们的示例中为$1,000$50$和$1,000$？🤷‍♂️。

If you’re interested read on, if you’re not, see yourself out. 🚪
 This article is the first of a series covering fundamental machine learning algorithms. Each post will be split into two parts
 The idea and key concepts- Most people should be able to follow this section and learn how the algorithm works 🤞
 The nitty gritty- This is for the interested reader and will include detailed mathematical derivations followed by an implementation in Python 🐍
   Regression is any algorithm that takes a collection of inputs and predicts an output. I’ll forgive you if you still don’t follow.
 Let’s give an example and this should make much more sense. Let’s say we are trying to predict how much a house 🏡 will sell for in a desirable area of North London. We may know a few facts about the house e.g. the square footage of the house 🏠, the square footage of the garden 🌳 and whether there is a garage 🚙.
 In machine learning we call these facts  variables or  features. Intuitively we may value the house using a combination of these features. 🏠🌳🚙
 Linear regression uses a  linear combination of the features to predict the output. This just means summing up each feature value multiplied by a number (called a  coefficient) to represent how important that feature is e.g
 $$\begin{aligned}\text{House Price} &= (£1000 * \text{🏠}) \\& + (£50 * \text{🌳}) \\& + (£1000 * \text{🚙?})\end{aligned}$$
 In this example the house price is calculated as £$1000$ for each square foot of the house plus $£50$ for each foot in the garden plus $£1000$ if there is a garage. If we took a nice $600$ square foot property with a small $500$ square foot garden and no garage our linear regression model would predict the house is worth $£625,000$.
 $$\begin{aligned}\text{House Price} &= (£1000 * \text{🏠}) + (£50 * \text{🌳}) + (£1000 * \text{🚙 ?}) \\&= (£1000 * 600) + (£50 * 500) + (£1000 * 0)\\&= £625,000\end{aligned}$$
 Now that’s how the linear regression model works! The question now is how do you choose the coefficients for the features, in our example the $1000$ the $50$ and the $1000$? 🤷‍♂️
 A common type of machine learning algorithm called  supervised learning algorithms find these parameters using  training data. Training data is just examples where you already know the answer. 随着2020年的到来和11月的临近，Facebook及其批评者正在努力解决该公司在即将到来的选举中扮演的角色--特别是它如何让唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)总统连任。就这家科技巨头而言，它最近的行动似乎公开招致了这些担忧，因为在首席执行官马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)主持保守派晚宴并听取彼得·泰尔(Peter Thiel)的建议时，它坚决拒绝对政治广告进行事实核查，从而招致了广泛的批评。在“纽约时报”周二公布的一份内部备忘录中，该公司的一名其他高管明确表示，Facebook显然对其政策如何促进特朗普的竞选活动漠不关心。在Facebook上发给员工的一篇内容广泛的帖子中，高管安德鲁·博斯沃思(Andrew Bosworth)发出了一个明确的信息：是的，我们可能确实在2016年帮助特朗普当选。我们不会做任何事来阻止他再次当选。
博斯沃思在2016年大选期间负责Facebook的广告工作，现在是其虚拟和增强现实部门的负责人，他在备忘录中涉及了广泛的话题，从社交媒体成瘾和媒体对Facebook的不公平对待，到2016年发生的事情。博斯沃思详细介绍了2016年俄罗斯的干预努力、虚假信息运动和剑桥分析丑闻，博斯沃思将其描述为一个“细节几乎都是错误的，但我认为审查基本上是正确的”。然而，这位Facebook高管最终将特朗普出人意料的胜利归功于这位当时的候选人的Facebook广告业务，博斯沃思将其描述为“数字广告活动的高水位线”。“那么，Facebook是唐纳德·特朗普当选的罪魁祸首吗？我认为答案是肯定的，但原因并非如此。“博斯沃思写道。“他当选不是因为俄罗斯，也不是因为错误的信息，也不是因为剑桥分析。他之所以当选，是因为他开展了我所见过的来自广告商的最好的一次数字广告宣传活动。句号。“。
当然，特朗普和他的竞选团队现在将希望复制这种取胜的广告策略，因为他们试图在2020年获得支持-借助Facebook的广告政策，该政策无法阻止这位臭名昭著的不诚实总统随心所欲地说出他想说的任何话。但在博斯沃思看来，Facebook不会也不应该阻止他。博斯沃思是一位“坚定的自由派”，他“不喜欢特朗普”，他写道，他意识到了Facebook的广告政策(这一政策也在2016年实施)“很可能会导致同样的结果”。他说，尽管如此，他还是决定关闭自己的冲动，“动用我可以动用的任何杠杆来避免同样的结果”。(注：博斯沃思是一名“坚定的自由主义者”，他“不喜欢特朗普”，他写道，他意识到Facebook的广告政策(这项政策也在2016年实施)“很可能会导致同样的结果”。
“此时此刻，我发现自己想起了”指环王“。博斯沃思写道：“特别是当弗罗多把戒指献给加拉德里亚尔时，她一开始想要公正地使用这一权力，但她知道这最终会让她堕落。”博斯沃思的比喻已经因为歪曲了这个经典故事而招致了一些批评。“尽管使用我们可用的工具来改变结果是很诱人的，但我相信我们绝不能这样做，否则我们将成为我们害怕的人。”
这位高管接着指出，尽管像“煽动暴力”或“压制选民”这样的情况对他来说会越界，但他最终反对“限制那些赢得受众的出版物的影响范围，尽管这些出版物的内容可能会让我反感，甚至是对我所珍视的道德哲学的反感。”博斯沃思写道：“如果我们限制人们可以获得的信息和他们可以说的话，那么我们就根本没有民主。”(在提供给“纽约时报”的一份声明中，博斯沃思表示，他的帖子“不是为公众撰写的”，但他“希望这篇帖子能鼓励我的同事继续优雅地接受批评，因为我们接受了监督我们平台的责任”。)。
博斯沃思是Facebook的坚定捍卫者，“泰晤士报”指出，在公司内部，博斯沃思被视为扎克伯格的“代理人”。在他的论点中，博斯沃思似乎呼应了这位首席执行官自己的想法。扎克伯格经常指出，需要“言论自由”作为Facebook备受争议的广告政策的理由，尽管批评家和硅谷的竞争对手抨击了这一思路。对于扎克伯格，显然对博斯沃思来说，阻止政客在竞选广告中公开撒谎等同于压制他们的言论，限制信息，选民应该能够通过他们在网上发布的东西来全面了解一位候选人-即使这包括发现他们足够不诚实，以至于会在竞选广告中撒谎。
虽然博斯沃思可能与扎克伯格的观点一致，但Facebook的许多其他员工持有截然不同的观点。几个月前，Facebook员工发表了一封公开信，声明他们反对这一广告政策，“泰晤士报”指出，许多人利用博斯沃思帖子上的评论提出了质疑

note_en:
As 2020 gets underway and November approaches, Facebook and its critics are grappling with the company's role in the impending election—and particularly how it could enable the reelection of President  Donald Trump. The tech giant, for its part, has been seemingly openly inviting those fears through its recent actions, drawing widespread criticism for adamantly refusing to fact-check political ads while CEO  Mark Zuckerberg hosts conservative  dinner parties and gets advice from   Peter Thiel. And in an  internal memo made  public by the  New York Times Tuesday, one of the company's other top execs made Facebook's apparent nonchalance toward how its policies facilitate Trump's campaign even more clear. In a wide-ranging Facebook post to employees, executive  Andrew Bosworth offered a clear message: Yes, we probably did help elect Trump in 2016. And we're not going to do anything to stop him from getting elected again.
 Bosworth, who was in charge of Facebook's advertising efforts during the 2016 election and now heads its virtual and augmented reality division, touches on a broad variety of topics in the memo, from social media addiction and the media's unfair treatment of Facebook, to what happened in 2016. Bosworth details the 2016 Russian interference efforts, misinformation campaigns, and the Cambridge Analytica scandal, which Bosworth described as a case “where the details are almost all wrong but I think the scrutiny is broadly right.” Ultimately, however, the Facebook exec credits Trump's surprising win to the then-candidate's Facebook ad operation, which Bosworth describes as “the high water mark of digital ad campaigns.” “So was Facebook responsible for Donald Trump getting elected? I think the answer is yes, but not for the reasons anyone thinks,” Bosworth writes. “He didn’t get elected because of Russia or misinformation or Cambridge Analytica. He got elected because he ran the single best digital ad campaign I’ve ever seen from any advertiser. Period.”
 Of course, Trump and his campaign will now be hoping to replicate that winning advertising strategy as it tries to garner support in 2020—with the help of a Facebook ad policy that can't stop the infamously dishonest president from saying whatever he wants. But in Bosworth's view, Facebook will not, and should not, stop him. Writing that he's realized Facebook's ad policy (which was also in place in 2016) “very well may lead to the same result” of Trump's victory, Bosworth—a “committed liberal” who's “no fan of Trump”—says he's nevertheless decided to shut down his impulse “to pull any lever at my disposal to avoid the same result.”
   “I find myself thinking of the Lord of the Rings at this moment. Specifically when Frodo offers the ring to Galadrial and she imagines using the power righteously, at first, but knows it will eventually corrupt her,” Bosworth wrote, making an analogy that's already garnered some criticism for  distorting the classic tale. “As tempting as it is to use the tools available to us to change the outcome, I am confident we must never do that or we will become that which we fear.”
  The exec went on to note that though situations like “incitement of violence” or “voter suppression” would cross the line for him, he's ultimately opposed to “limiting the reach of publications who have earned their audience, as distasteful as their content may be to me and even to the moral philosophy I hold so dear.” “If we limit what information people have access to and what they can say then we have no democracy at all,” Bosworth wrote. (In a statement provided to the  Times, Bosworth said his post “wasn’t written for public consumption,” but that he “hoped this post would encourage my co-workers to continue to accept criticism with grace as we accept the responsibility we have overseeing our platform.”)
  Bosworth—a staunch  defender of Facebook whom the  Times notes is viewed internally at the company as a “proxy” for Zuckerberg—appears to be echoing the CEO's own thinking in his argument here. Zuckerberg has frequently pointed to the need for “free speech” as the  justification for Facebook's controversial ad policy, even as critics and  Silicon Valley rivals lambast this line of thinking. To Zuckerberg, and apparently Bosworth, stopping politicians from openly lying in their campaign ads would be tantamount to silencing their speech and restricting information, and voters should be able to get a complete picture of a candidate through what they publish online—even if that includes finding out that they're dishonest enough to lie in campaign ads.
 While Bosworth may be in line with Zuckerberg's views, however, many other Facebook employees hold a much different opinion. Months after Facebook employees published an  open letter declaring their objection to the ad policy, the  Times notes that many used the comments on Bosworth's post to take issue with his views, pointing out how his line of thinking could lead to Facebook promoting the rise of populist leaders worldwide, and noting that the company was holding politicians to a different standard than all other Facebook users, whose posts are screened for misinformation. In a public  Facebook post responding to the  Times's publication of his memo, Bosworth noted that his post received “dozens of comments challenging some of my statements and exploring the implication of others,” and “several of those comment threads changed my views.” Whether this “very healthy back and forth” will be enough to change Facebook's policies, however, remains to be seen.

Facebook首席运营官谢丽尔·桑德伯格(Sheryl Sandberg)在一篇博客文章中表示，Facebook将花费2亿美元支持美国黑人拥有的企业和组织。这是Facebook今天宣布的支持黑人社区的几项倡议之一。
桑德伯格说，该公司还将在Facebook应用程序中增加一个部分，以黑人的故事、反对种族不公正的事业筹款活动和教育资源为特色。
Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格已经在5月31日表示，Facebook将向“致力于种族正义的团体”承诺1000万美元，但他指出，该公司正在与顾问和员工合作，研究如何最好地分配这笔钱。
该平台过去曾因允许歧视而受到审查。例如，2016年ProPublica的一项调查发现，Facebook的广告商可以选择将特定种族的人排除在广告之外。该工具如果与房屋广告一起使用，可能会违反公平住房法，住房和城市发展部在2019年3月指控Facebook违反了这一规定。白人至上主义者也在这个平台上蓬勃发展，尽管Facebook宣布禁止他们。
为黑人所有的企业安排的2亿美元将被分成两个主要部分。桑德伯格在她的博客文章中说，一半将投资于黑人拥有的小企业、黑人创造者和与黑人社区合作的非营利组织。在这1亿美元中，2500万美元将拨给黑人内容创作者，而7500万美元将是支持小企业和非营利组织的“现金和广告积分”。
桑德伯格表示，其余1亿美元将流向黑人所有的供应商，“从设施到建筑，再到营销机构等等。”该公司还承诺每年与黑人拥有的供应商合作1亿美元。此外，桑德伯格说，该公司制定了一个目标，从明年开始，每年与“不同供应商”的支出至少为10亿美元。
为了纪念1865年得克萨斯州被奴役的人们获得自由的日子-6月19日，Facebook将向Facebook上超过25万名筹款人捐赠500万美元，支持平等正义倡议、瑟古德·马歇尔学院基金和天真项目。
桑德伯格说，Facebook应用程序的新部分名为“提升黑人声音”，将“突出黑人的故事，分享教育资源，并通过为种族正义事业筹款来激励人们采取行动。”该公司还计划在Instagram Search中公开账户，以“帮助人们为种族正义采取行动”，尽管桑德伯格的帖子没有进一步详细说明这意味着什么。
此外，桑德伯格说，Facebook计划在未来五年通过增加30%的有色人种来增加领导层的多样性，其中包括30%的黑人。该公司还承诺到2023年底将黑人和拉丁裔员工的数量翻一番。Facebook之前宣布了一个目标，到同一天，其员工中有50%由“代表性不足的社区”组成。
其他科技巨头也宣布了对种族平等的九位数财务承诺。上周，谷歌承诺提供1.75亿美元支持黑人所有的企业、黑人企业家等。苹果公司上周启动了1亿美元的种族公平和正义倡议，其中包括增加与黑人拥有的供应链合作伙伴的支出。
note_en:
Facebook will spend $200 million to support black-owned businesses and organizations in the US, COO Sheryl Sandberg said in a blog post. It is one of  several Facebook initiatives announced today to support black communities.
 The company also will add a section to the Facebook app that features stories from black people, fundraisers for causes fighting racial injustice, and educational resources, Sandberg said.
 CEO Mark Zuckerberg had already said  on May 31st that Facebook would commit $10 million to “groups working on racial justice,” though noted that the company was working with advisors and employees to figure out how to best distribute the money.
 The platform has come under scrutiny for enabling discrimination in the past. For example, a 2016   ProPublica investigation found that Facebook advertisers could choose to exclude people of a certain race from seeing ads. That tool, if used with housing ads, could violate the Fair Housing Act, and the Department of Housing and Urban Development charged Facebook for that violation  in March 2019. White supremacists have also flourished  on the platform, despite Facebook’s announced ban on them.
 The $200 million slated for black-owned businesses will be split into two main buckets. Half will be invested in black-owned small businesses, black creators, and nonprofits working with black communities, Sandberg said in her blog post. Of that $100 million, $25 million will go to black content creators, while $75 million will be “grants of cash and ad credits” to support the small businesses and nonprofits.
 The other $100 million will go to black-owned suppliers, “from facilities to construction to marketing agencies and more,” according to Sandberg. The company is also committing to spend $100 million with black-owned suppliers every year. In addition, the company is making a goal to spend at least $1 billion with “diverse suppliers” annually starting next year, Sandberg said.
 In honor of  Juneteenth, the holiday that celebrates the day in 1865 when enslaved people in Texas learned they were free, Facebook will donate $5 million to more than 250,000 fundraisers on Facebook supporting the Equal Justice Initiative, the Thurgood Marshall College Fund and the Innocence Project.
 The new section of the Facebook app, called “Lift Black Voices,” will “highlight stories from black people, share educational resources, and inspire people to take action through fundraising for racial justice causes,” Sandberg says. The company also plans to surface accounts in Instagram search to “help people take action for racial justice,” though Sandberg’s post didn’t elaborate further on what that meant.
   In addition, Facebook plans to increase diversity in its leadership ranks over the next five years by adding 30 percent more people of color, including 30 percent more Black people, Sandberg said. The company also committed to double the number of black and Latinx staff by the end of 2023. Facebook had previously announced a goal to have 50 percent of its workforce made up of “underrepresented communities” by that same date.
 Other tech giants have also announced nine-digit financial commitments toward racial equity.  Google committed $175 million to support black-owned businesses, black entrepreneurs, and more last week. And Apple launched the $100 million Racial Equity and Justice Initiative  last week which, among other things, includes increasing spending with black-owned supply chain partners.
title: Facebook承诺2亿美元支持黑人拥有的企业
title_en: Facebook commits $200M to support black-owned businesses

每年夏天，随着白天变得漫长，阿纳斯塔西娅·马卡里耶娃(Anastassia Makarieva)会离开她在圣彼得堡的实验室，前往俄罗斯北部广袤的森林度假。这位核物理学家在白海岸边的云杉和松树之间扎营，沿着该地区宽阔的河流划着皮划艇，记录自然和天气。“森林是我内心生活的重要组成部分，”她说。在她每年一度的北上朝圣的25年里，它们也成为她职业生活中很重要的一部分。
十多年来，马卡里耶娃一直在倡导一种理论，该理论是与她在彼得堡核物理研究所(PNPI)的导师兼同事维克多·戈尔什科夫(Victor Gorshkov)共同开发的，该理论关于俄罗斯的北方森林-地球上最大的树木-如何调节北亚的气候。这是一种简单的物理学，有着深远的影响，描述了树木呼出的水蒸气是如何驱动风的：穿过欧洲大陆的风，将潮湿的空气从欧洲带到西伯利亚，然后进入蒙古和中国；风带来降雨，让东西伯利亚的大河保持流动；风给中国北部平原浇水，那里是地球上人口最多的国家的粮仓。
凭借其吸收二氧化碳和呼出氧气的能力，世界上的大森林通常被称为地球的肺。但去年去世的马卡里耶娃和戈尔什科夫表示，他们也是它跳动的心脏。“森林是复杂的自我维持的造雨系统，也是地球大气循环的主要驱动力，”马卡里耶娃说。它们将大量的水分循环到空气中，在这个过程中，还会激起风，将水泵送到世界各地。这个想法的第一部分-森林造雨-起源于其他科学家，在森林砍伐猖獗的世界里，越来越受到水资源管理者的赞赏。但第二部分，马卡里耶娃称之为生物泵的理论，更具争议性。
这项工作的理论基础已经发表在不太知名的期刊上，马卡里耶娃也得到了一小群同事的支持。但生物水泵面临着批评的逆风，特别是来自气候建模人员的批评，他们中的一些人说它的影响可以忽略不计，并完全驳斥了这个想法。这场争论让马卡里耶娃成为了一个局外人：在模型师的世界里，她是一名理论物理学家；在西方科学家领导的领域里，她是一名俄罗斯人；在一个由男性主导的领域里，她是一名女性。
然而，如果这个想法是正确的，这个想法可能有助于解释为什么尽管远离海洋，但偏远的有森林的大陆的内部接收的雨水与海岸一样多-以及为什么没有森林的大陆的内部往往是干旱的。这也意味着，从俄罗斯泰加到亚马逊热带雨林的森林并不只生长在气候适宜的地方。它们也会影响天气。挪威生命科学大学的森林生态学家道格拉斯·谢尔说：“到目前为止，我所了解到的一切都表明，生物泵是正确的。”在世界森林的未来充满疑问的情况下，“即使我们认为这一理论只有很小的可能性是正确的，但了解其中的一种或另一种方式将是极其重要的。”
许多气象学教科书仍然教授水循环的漫画，海洋蒸发是大部分大气水分凝结在云中并以雨的形式降落的原因。这幅图忽略了植被的作用，特别是树木，它们的作用就像巨大的饮水机。它们的根从土壤中吸收水分进行光合作用，叶子中的微小孔隙将未使用的水分以蒸汽的形式释放到空气中。这个过程，在树上相当于出汗，被称为蒸腾作用。这样，一棵成熟的树一天可以释放数百升水。由于树叶为交换提供了充足的表面积，森林通常比同样大小的水体的蒸发量能向空气中输送更多的水分。
飞河湿空气蒸腾冷凝冷凝蒸腾。雨天游行，即所谓的飞扬的河流，是盛行的风，它吸收森林呼出的水蒸气，将雨水送到远处的流域。一种有争议的理论认为，森林本身驱动着风(见下图)。生物泵理论表明，森林不仅能造雨，还能造风。当沿海森林上空的水蒸气凝结时，它会降低大气压力，产生风，将潮湿的海洋空气吸引进来。蒸腾和凝结的循环可以形成风，将降雨带到内陆数千公里处。多亏了一条横跨西伯利亚的飞扬的河流，中国大约80%的降雨来自西部。亚马逊的飞河为南美东南部提供了70%的降雨。播种风向。
重要的是
到这个时候，气象学家正在森林上空追踪到一股大气急流，高度约为1.5公里。被称为南美低空喷气式飞机，风从东向西吹过亚马逊，速度大约和赛车一样快，然后安第斯山脉将它们转向南。萨拉蒂和其他人猜测，这架喷气式飞机携带了大部分泄漏的水分，并将其称为“飞行的河流”。巴西国家空间研究所的气候研究员安东尼奥·诺布雷(Antonio Nobre)说，亚马逊飞河现在被认为携带的水量与它下面的巨大陆河一样多。
多年来，人们认为飞扬的河流仅限于亚马逊地区。上世纪90年代，代尔夫特理工大学(Delft University Of Technology)的水文学家休伯特·萨文尼耶(Hubert Savenije)开始研究西非的水分循环。使用基于天气数据的水文模型，他发现，当人们从海岸向内陆移动时，来自森林的降雨量比例增加，内陆达到90%。这一发现有助于解释为什么在过去的半个世纪里，随着沿海森林的消失，萨赫勒内陆地区变得更加干燥。
Savenije的一名学生Ruud van der Ent进一步提出了这个想法，创建了一个空气湿气流动的全球模型。他将降雨、湿度、风速和温度的观测数据与蒸发和蒸腾的理论估计相结合，创建了第一个比河流流域规模更大的湿气流动模型。
2010年，范德恩特和他的同事们报告了该模型的结论：在全球范围内，40%的降水来自陆地，而不是海洋。通常情况下，它是更多的。亚马逊的飞河提供了里奥德拉普拉塔盆地70%的降雨量，该盆地横跨南美洲东南部。范德恩特最惊讶地发现，中国80%的水来自西方，其中大部分是由斯堪的纳维亚和俄罗斯的北方森林循环利用的大西洋湿气。这一旅程包括几个阶段-蒸腾周期，然后是顺风向雨，然后是蒸腾-需要6个月或更长时间。“这与你在高中学到的以前的知识相矛盾，”他说。中国毗邻太平洋，但它的大部分降雨是从遥远的西部陆地循环过来的湿气。
如果马卡里耶娃是正确的，森林不仅提供水分，还提供携带水分的风。
四分之一个世纪以来，她与戈尔什科夫一起在PNPI工作，最初是他的学生。PNPI是俄罗斯最重要的民用和军用核研究机构库尔恰托夫研究所(Kurchat Ov Institute)的一部分。他们从一开始就是特立独行的人，在一个到处都是物理学家的地方研究生态学，这些物理学家使用核反应堆的中子束来研究材料。她说，作为理论家，他们拥有“非凡的研究和思想自由”，无论走到哪里，他们都会追求大气物理学。“维克多教会我：什么都不要害怕，”她说。
2007年，在“水文学和地球系统科学”杂志上，他们首次概述了他们对生物泵的愿景。这从一开始就具有挑衅性，因为它与气象学的一个长期信条相矛盾：风在很大程度上是由大气的差异加热驱动的。当暖空气上升时，它降低了下面的气压，实际上在空气进入的表面创造了空间。例如，在夏天，陆地表面往往加热得更快，并从较冷的海洋吸收潮湿的微风。
马卡里耶娃和戈尔什科夫认为，第二个过程有时可能占主导地位。当森林中的水蒸气凝结成云时，气体就会变成体积较小的液体。这降低了空气压力，并从凝结较少的地区水平吸入空气。在实践中，这意味着沿海森林上空的凝结会推动海风，将潮湿的空气吸入内陆，在那里最终会凝结并以雨的形式降落。如果森林继续向内陆蔓延，这个循环就会继续下去，使湿润的风持续数千公里。
该理论颠覆了传统思维：驱动水循环的不是大气环流，而是驱动空气质量循环的水循环。
谢尔在十多年前成为这一理论的支持者，他认为这是对飞河想法的点缀。“它们并不是相互排斥的，”他说。“这个水泵解释了河流的力量。”他说，生物泵可以解释“冰冷的亚马逊悖论”。从1月到6月，当亚马逊盆地比海洋更冷的时候，强风从大西洋吹到亚马逊-如果它们是由差异加热造成的，预计会出现相反的情况。另一位早期的追随者诺布尔(Nobre)热情洋溢地说：“他们不是从数据开始的，他们是从基本原则开始的。”
即使是那些怀疑这一理论的人也同意，森林丧失可能会对气候产生深远的影响。许多科学家认为，数千年前的森林砍伐是澳大利亚内陆和西非沙漠化的罪魁祸首。令人担忧的是，未来的森林砍伐可能会使其他地区干涸，例如，亚马逊雨林的部分地区将变成稀树草原。科罗拉多州立大学柯林斯堡分校的大气化学家帕特里克·凯斯(Patrick Keys)说，中国、非洲萨赫勒和阿根廷潘帕斯的农业区也处于危险之中。
2018年，凯斯和他的同事使用了一个类似于范德恩特的模型，追踪了29个全球特大城市的降雨源。他发现，19个地区的大部分供水高度依赖遥远的森林，包括巴基斯坦的卡拉奇，中国的武汉和上海，以及印度的新德里和加尔各答。他说：“即使是迎风向土地利用变化引起的降水量的微小变化，也可能对城市供水的脆弱性产生重大影响。”
一些模型甚至表明，通过去除湿气来源，森林砍伐可能会改变河流飞行路径以外的天气模式。迈阿密大学(University Of Miami)的气候学家罗尼·阿维萨尔(Roni Avissar)对这种远程联系进行了建模，他说，就像热带太平洋洋流和风向的变化厄尔尼诺现象通过“远程联系”影响遥远地区的天气一样，亚马逊砍伐森林也会减少美国中西部的降雨量，减少内华达山脉的积雪。牵强吗？“一点也不，”他说。“我们知道厄尔尼诺现象可以做到这一点，因为与砍伐森林不同，厄尔尼诺现象会反复出现，我们可以看到这种模式。两者都是由投射到大气中的温度和湿度的微小变化造成的。“。
斯德哥尔摩大学研究土地、水和气候之间相互作用的兰王-厄兰德松(Lan Wang-Erlandsson)表示，现在是水资源管理者将重点从流域内的水和土地利用转移到流域外发生的土地利用变化的时候了。“我们需要新的国际水文协议来维护源头地区的森林，”她说。
两年前，在各国政府都参加的联合国森林论坛的一次会议上，伯尔尼大学的土地研究员大卫·埃里森(David Ellison)提出了一个恰当的例子：一项研究显示，尼罗河的主要源头埃塞俄比亚高地总降雨量的40%来自刚果盆地森林中循环的水分。埃及、苏丹和埃塞俄比亚正在就共享尼罗河水域进行谈判，这是一项拖延已久的协议。但埃里森表示，如果远离这三个国家的刚果盆地的森林砍伐导致湿气来源枯竭，这样的协议将一文不值。“在全球淡水资源的管理中，森林和水之间的相互作用几乎完全被忽视了。”
生物泵将进一步提高赌注，因为它表明森林损失不仅改变了湿润来源，还改变了风向。埃里森警告说，如果这一理论是正确的，将“对行星空气循环模式产生至关重要的影响”，特别是那些将潮湿空气带到大陆内部的理论。
这一理论的支持者可能是少数。2010年，Makarieva、Gorshkov、Sheil、Nobre和加州大学河滨分校(University of California，Riverside)的生态学家李柏莲(Bai-Lian Li)向开放同行评议的主要期刊《大气化学与物理》(The Air Chemical And Physical)提交了一份本应是对生物泵的里程碑式描述。题为“风从何而来？”这篇论文在网上遭到了铺天盖地的批评，该杂志花了几个月的时间才找到两名愿意对其进行评论的科学家。普林斯顿大学地球物理流体动力学实验室的气象学家艾萨克·赫尔德(Isaac Hold)最终自愿-并建议拒绝。“这不是一种神秘的效应，”他说。“它很小，包含在一些大气模型中。”批评人士说，水蒸气冷凝时释放的热量导致空气膨胀，抵消了冷凝创造空间的效果。但马卡里耶娃说，这两种影响在空间上是分开的，变暖效应发生在高空，凝结压降发生在更接近地表的地方，在那里产生了生物风。
另一位评论家是朱迪思·库里(Judith Curry)，他当时是佐治亚理工学院的大气物理学家，长期以来一直对气候模型核心的大气动力学感到担忧。她认为发表这篇论文很重要，并表示这种僵局“对气候科学非常不利，它非常需要核心物理学家的注入”。经过3年的辩论，该杂志的编辑否决了霍尔德的建议，并发表了这篇论文，称发表这篇论文“不是为了支持”，而是“为了促进关于这一有争议的理论的科学对话的继续，这可能会导致反证或验证。”
从那时起，既没有证实也没有反驳，但在很大程度上是一种僵局。哥伦比亚大学的气候模型师加文·施密特(Gavin Schmidt)说，“这简直是胡说八道。”两位作者对批评的回应是“实际上只是数学，没有人相信继续对话有任何意义。”巴西气象学家、国家自然灾害监测和预警中心主任何塞·马伦戈说：“我认为抽水机是存在的，但现在这只是理论上的。气候模型界还没有接受它，但俄罗斯人是世界上最好的理论家，所以我们需要适当的实地实验来检验它。“。然而，包括马卡里耶娃在内，还没有人明确提出这样的测试可能是什么样子的。
就马卡里耶娃而言，她是在这一理论的基础上发展起来的，她在最近的一系列论文中辩称，同样的机制也会影响热带气旋，热带气旋是由海洋上空水汽凝结时释放的热量驱动的。在2017年发表在《大气研究》杂志上的一篇论文中，她和她的同事们提出，陆地上森林设置的生物泵将富含水分的空气从气旋苗圃中抽走。她说，这可能解释了为什么南大西洋很少形成气旋：亚马逊和刚果热带雨林之间的雨林吸收了如此多的水分，以至于剩下的为飓风提供燃料的东西太少了。
麻省理工学院(Massachusetts Institute Of Technology)主要飓风研究员克里·伊曼纽尔(Kerry Emanuel)表示，拟议中的影响“虽然不可忽略，但非常小”。他更喜欢对没有南大西洋飓风的其他解释，比如该地区的凉爽海水给空气带来的湿气较少，以及它的强烈切变风破坏了龙卷风的形成。马卡里耶娃同样对传统论者不屑一顾，他说，一些现有的飓风强度理论“与热力学定律相冲突”。她还有另一篇关于这个主题的论文在美国大气科学杂志上接受同行评审。“我们担心，尽管有编辑的鼓励，我们的作品还是会再次遭到拒绝，”她说。
即使马卡里耶娃的想法在西方是边缘的，但它们正在俄罗斯扎根。去年，政府开始了一场公众对话，以修改其森林法。除了严格保护的地区，俄罗斯的森林对商业开发是开放的，但政府和联邦林业局正在考虑将其命名为“气候保护林”。“我们林业部门的一些代表对生物水泵印象深刻，想要引入一个新的类别，”她说。这个想法得到了俄罗斯科学院的支持。马卡里耶娃说，成为共识的一部分，而不是长期的局外人标志着一种变化。
今年夏天，冠状病毒的封锁扼杀了她一年一度的北部森林之旅。回到圣彼得堡，她已经静下心来回应匿名同行评论家对她作品的又一轮反对。她坚称泵理论最终会占上风。“科学有一种天然的惰性，”她说。她带着一种俄罗斯的黑色幽默，引用了德国传奇物理学家马克斯·普朗克(Max Planck)的话，据说普朗克曾说过，科学“一次推进一个葬礼”。
